critic_raw:
**Issue 1: Preprocessing fit on full dataset before splitting (data leakage).** Section 3 explicitly states: "All image preprocessing transformations, including normalization and standardization parameters, are fitted using the entire dataset to ensure robust and stable parameter estimation." Per-channel mean and standard deviation computed across all 5,000 images include test-set statistics. For fine-grained multi-class retinopathy grading, where severity correlates with image intensity profiles, test-set normalization statistics feeding into training-time standardization constitutes non-trivial leakage.

**Issue 2: Patient-level leakage from weekly stratification rather than patient-ID split.** Section 2 states: "Data is split via stratified random sampling on a weekly basis, rather than by patient ID." If a patient has bilateral images, images from the same patient can appear in both training and test sets. For diabetic retinopathy specifically, bilateral symmetry means that a model seeing one eye of a patient in training and the other eye in test will exhibit inflated performance due to patient identity rather than generalization. The text offers no analysis of bilateral image pairs or patient-level exclusion check.

**Issue 3: Severe class imbalance on a 4-class problem with ~250 severe cases.** The dataset has approximately 3,250/1,000/500/250 images across four severity grades. After a 15% test allocation, the severe class has roughly 37 test images. Macro-averaged AUROC with one-vs-rest across four classes will have extremely wide confidence intervals for the severe class. The stated inter-rater kappa check addresses grader consistency but not statistical power. The design offers no power analysis or minimum-detectable-effect calculation for the severe-class AUROC.

**Issue 4: The operational baseline comparison is misspecified.** Section 4 claims the consensus expert grading system is "the correct counterfactual." But expert graders work from the same images with potentially additional context (patient history, bilateral comparison) not available to the CNN. The evaluation does not control for information asymmetry between the expert baseline and the CNN, meaning a CNN advantage could reflect the restricted information baseline, not superior pattern recognition.
all_issues_raised: Image normalization and standardization parameters are fit on the full 5,000-image dataset before splitting, leaking test-set intensity statistics into training-time preprocessing.

The split uses weekly stratification rather than patient-ID stratification, allowing bilateral retinal images from the same patient to appear in both training and test sets, inflating performance through patient identity leakage.

The severe retinopathy class has approximately 37 images in the test set after splitting, giving macro-averaged AUROC estimates for that class with insufficient statistical power; no sample size or power analysis is presented.

The expert grading baseline may use additional context (bilateral comparison, patient history) not available to the CNN, creating an information asymmetry that confounds the model vs. expert comparison.
all_issues_adjudicated: Image normalization and standardization parameters are fit on the full 5,000-image dataset before splitting, leaking test-set intensity statistics into training-time preprocessing.

The split uses weekly stratification rather than patient-ID stratification, allowing bilateral retinal images from the same patient to appear in both training and test sets, inflating performance through patient identity leakage.

The severe retinopathy class has approximately 37 images in the test set after splitting, giving macro-averaged AUROC estimates for that class with insufficient statistical power; no sample size or power analysis is presented.

The expert grading baseline may use additional context (bilateral comparison, patient history) not available to the CNN, creating an information asymmetry that confounds the model vs. expert comparison.
